coredata.dc:title: Superior initial coulombic efficiency through graphene quantum dot decorated on MoS2
coredata.dc:creator: Sim, Glenn Joey
Huang, Zhi Xiang
Wang, Ye
Kong, Dezhi
Huang, Shaozhuan
Liu, Bo
Yang, Hui Ying
coredata.dc:description: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) nanoflower was grown onto 3D graphene (3DG) by a simple hydrothermal method. Subsequently, Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were decorated on the surface of MoS2 to further enhance the electrochemical performance through a one-step electrodeposition method. The NGQDs decorated MoS2 on 3DG (NGQDs@MoS2/3DG) is further employed directly as a binder free anode of sodium ion batteries (SIBs). NGQD@MoS2/3DG nanoarchitecture delivers a specific capacity of 638 mA h g−1 at 50 mA g−1, and an ultra-high first cycle coulombic efficiency of 85.4%. The outstanding Na+ storage properties of NGQD@MoS2/3DG was attributed to the synergistic effect among the conductive 3DG carbon matrix, MoS2 nanoflowers and decorated NGQDs. These results obtained potentially unveil a path for the development of excellent electrochemical performance with high initial coulombic efficiency of SIBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3D graphene
Molybdenum disulfide
Graphene quantum dots
Sodium ion batteries

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Singapore Ministry of Education Academic Research Fund Tier 2 ( MOE2015-T2-1-150 ). Y. Wang acknowledges the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21603192 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of the growth process of 3DG via CVD, MoS2/3DG via hydrothermal and NGQD@MoS2/3DG via electrodeposition. Low and high (inset) magnification of SEM images of (b) 3DG, (c) MoS2/3DG and (d) NGQDs@MoS2/3DG.

HRTEM images of (a) low magnification of MoS2/3DG and (b) high magnification of MoS2/3DG with inset showing lattice of MoS2 (c) low magnification of MoS2@NGQD/3DG and (d) high magnification of NGQD@MoS2/3DG, inset showing diameter of NGQD.
(a) XRD pattern of NGQD@MoS2/3DG, MoS2/3DG and 3DG. (d) Raman spectra of NGQD@MoS2/3DG, MoS2/3DG, 3DG.

Cyclic voltammograms of (a) NGQD@MoS2/3DG electrode and (b) MoS2/3DG at a rate of 0.05 mV s−1 in a potential range of 0.01–3.0 V. Galvanostatic discharge and charge curves of (c) NGQD@MoS2/3DG and (d) MoS2/3DG electrodes in the potential range of 0.01–3.0 V. (e) Rate capability of NGQD@MoS2/3DG, MoS2/3DG and 3DG electrodes. (f) Cycling performance of NGQD@MoS2/3DG, MoS2/3DG and 3DG electrodes at a current density of 500 mA g−1, and the corresponding coulombic efficiency of NGQD@MoS2/3DG electrode. The first 5 cycles are for activation process with a current density of 50 mA g−1.
Nyquist impedance spectra of NGQD@MoS2/3DG and MoS2/3DG electrodes. (a) in the full range of Z′ from 0 to 3000 Ω. (b) in the region of Z′ from 0 to 700 Ω. Inset showing the model of the EIS circuit.
Fitting results of the EIS spectra using the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 5(b).

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Conflicts of interest: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

head.ce:title: Superior initial coulombic efficiency through graphene quantum dot decorated on MoS2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pillar of Engineering Product Development, Singapore University of Technology and Design, 8 Somapah Road, Singapore 487372, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Singapore University of Technology and Design

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Airbus Group Innovations Singapore, 110 Seletar Aerospace View, Singapore 797562, Singapore

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Materials Physics of Ministry of Education, Department of Physics and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450052, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Materials Physics of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) nanoflower was grown onto 3D graphene (3DG) by a simple hydrothermal method. Subsequently, Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were decorated on the surface of MoS2 to further enhance the electrochemical performance through a one-step electrodeposition method. The NGQDs decorated MoS2 on 3DG (NGQDs@MoS2/3DG) is further employed directly as a binder free anode of sodium ion batteries (SIBs). NGQD@MoS2/3DG nanoarchitecture delivers a specific capacity of 638 mA h g−1 at 50 mA g−1, and an ultra-high first cycle coulombic efficiency of 85.4%. The outstanding Na+ storage properties of NGQD@MoS2/3DG was attributed to the synergistic effect among the conductive 3DG carbon matrix, MoS2 nanoflowers and decorated NGQDs. These results obtained potentially unveil a path for the development of excellent electrochemical performance with high initial coulombic efficiency of SIBs.

ce:section.ce:para: The exponential rate is advancing, which inevitably brings about the increase in demand of energy supply to portable electronic devices and electronic vehicles [1–6]. As a result, high performance energy storage devices have attracted much interests both in industrial and research communities [7–11]. Recently, sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have gained much attention due to their large natural abundance and low cost of sodium salts [4,12,13]. Moreover, SIB is a suitable alternative to lithium ion battery (LIB) as it has similar insertion chemistry with lithium ions [14,15]. Carbon based anode materials, metal oxides, and metal sulfides have been extensively studied as promising anode materials for SIBs [16]. As the ionic radius of sodium ions (∼0.106 nm) are larger than that of lithium ions (∼0.076 nm), SIBs anode materials suffered with various problems including large polarization, volume expansion and slow reaction kinetics, resulting in low reversible capacities and inferior cycle life [17]. Therefore, it is challenging to find a suitable anode material that would possess a high rate performance and long cycle life for SIBs [4,18].

Two-dimensional (2D) molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) has the covalently bonded X-M-X layers that interact by weak van der Waals forces with larger interlayer space which makes it a suitable candidate for anode material in SIBs [19]. According to the conversion reaction:
(1)
N
a x
M o
S 2
+
4 - x
N
a +
+ 4
e -
→ 2 N
a 2
S + M o

One MoS2 molecule would react with four sodium ions to give rise to a theoretical capacity of 670 mA h g−1 [20]. MoS2 anodes suffer from low capacity and inferior cycle life due to the relative low conductivity and large volume change during the sodiation/desodiation process [21]. Various methods have been applied to improve the conductivity and reduce the volume change during the sodiation/desodiation process. For example, Liu et al. embedded Tin nanoparticles homogeneously in carbon network to alleviate and suppress the volume change during the sodiation and desodiation process, which solved problems of pulverization [22]. However, the electrochemical performance (rate capability and initial coulombic efficiency) of MoS2 based anode materials still need to be further improved for real applications. Understanding the fundamental sodiation and desodiation processes is the most important in overcoming the main challenges of low initial coulombic efficiency (CE) due to the irreversible capacity loss during the first charge/discharge cycle [23,24].

In this study, flower-shaped MoS2 was grown on the conductive three-dimensional graphene (3DG) carbon matrix, followed by decoration NGQDs on the surface of MoS2 to form the NGQDs@MoS2/3DG hybrid nanostructures. 3DG is employed as the substrate material of the anode owing to its extraordinary properties, such as high specific surface area and good electrical conductivity [25]. NGQDs (<10 nm) used as effective sensitizer and stabilizer to modify the surface chemistry of the active materials of SIBs [26]. The homogeneous NGQDs covering can effectively activate more sites in the MoS2 layer to provide extra Na+ storage venues during long cycling, and an improvement in the initial CE. The as-synthesized NGQD@MoS2/3DG nanoarchitecture exhibits high rate capability and cycling stability. It can deliver an improved specific capacity of 638 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1 and enhanced initial CE of 85.4%.

All chemicals (Pyridine, L-cystine, Sodium molybdate dihydrate, Citric Acid and Urea) were used as it is when received from Sigma Aldrich. 1.2 mm thick Nickel foam is purchased directly from Alantum Advanced Technology Materials (Shenyang).

The Ni foam with the size of 1.50 × 4.5 cm was rolled and placed into a 1-inch quartz tube. Before heating, N2/H2 mixed gas (95%/5%) was passed through the quartz tube for 10 min, removing all residual air in the tube and maintaining an inert environment. Thereafter, a heat rate of 50 °C/min was applied to heat the quartz tube up till 1000 °C. At this temperature, pyridine was transported by the flowing N2/H2 gas through the bubbling of pyridine for 8 mins for the growth of nitrogen-doped 3DG on the surface of nickel foam. The quartz tube was left to cool to room temperature rapidly (∼100 °C/min). Subsequently, the Ni foam on 3DG-Ni was removed in an etchant solution of 3 M HCl at 80 °C for 3 h. Pure 3DG was washed with deionized (DI) water and ethanol several times before drying at 60 °C. The average loading yields of 3DG is ∼0.5 mg/cm2.

L-cysteine (0.25 mM) and Na₂MoO₄·2H₂O (0.03 mM) was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized (DI) water and 15 mL of ethanol. Then, 6 pieces of 3DG (12 mm in diameter) were placed into the mixture and transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-line stainless steel autoclave. The stainless-steel autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The synthesized MoS2/3DG was collected by rinsing with DI water and ethanol for several times and dried at 60 °C for 4 h. The loading of MoS2 on 3DG was between 2.0 and 2.5 mg/cm2.

Citric acid (0.21 g, 1 mmol) and urea (0.18 g, 3 mmol) were added to 5 mL of water and stirred thoroughly to form a completely clear solution. This solution was poured into a Teflon autoclave, sealed and heated to 160 °C for 4 h. The product was washed and centrifuged several times, following by re-dispersing in water with a concentration of 1 mg/ml. The NGQD@MoS2/3DG electrode was prepared by a simple electro-deposition method with a two-electrode setup. MoS2/3DG composite and Pt foil electrode were used as the working and counter electrode, respectively. An aqueous precursor solution with 0.1 mL NGQDs dispersed in deionized water (40 mL) was used as the electrolyte, and the deposition was performed at a constant potential of 6 V for 5 min. After the deposition, the working electrode was washed with distilled water and then annealed at 450 °C in the mix gas N2/H2 (95%/5%) environment for 2 h.

The morphology of the synthesis was carried out under field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JSM-7600) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2100F). The crystalline phase and structure of the synthesis were identified by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8). A confocal Raman setup with a 532 nm laser excitation (WITec Instruments Corp, Germany) was used to conduct Raman spectroscopy on the samples. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Shimadzu, DTG-60) was used to measure the nanocomposite content.

The above synthesized NGQD@MoS2/3DG was used directly as the anode material in the two-electrode half-cell configuration where sodium metal acts as the counter electrode. All working electrodes were dried at 120 °C for 12 h before assembly. The coin cell CR 2032was assembled in a glove box filled with argon gas with the oxygen and moisture level lower than 1 ppm. Each coin cell consisted of a glass Fiber Filter (GB-100R, Advantec, Japan) membrane sandwiched between a working electrode (active material) and counter electrode (sodium metal). The sodium electrode was prepared by cutting a sodium ingot in the glove box. 1 mol NaPF6 dissolved into EC/DEC solution (1:1, v:v) with 2% fluoroethylene-carbonate (FEC, stabilizer) was used as the electrolyte. Electrochemical measurements were carried out after 24 h of battery assembly. CV and EIS measurements were performed by an electrochemical workstation (VMP3, Bio-Logic, Claix, France). The galvanostatic charge/discharge test was measured in the voltage range of 0.01–3 V at various current densities ranging from 50 to 1000 mA g−1 using a battery analyzer (Neware, Shenzhen, China).

The step wise synthesis process and morphology of NGQD@MoS2/3DG nanostructure is presented in the illustration and the corresponding SEM images in Figs. 1 and S1. 3DG was grown on the surface of nickel foam in the 1st step. Carbon network with smooth surface connected to form spatial three-dimensional network with plenty of millimeters pores (Fig. 1b). After etching away of nickel foam, 3DG was placed in the Teflon lined autoclave with the dissolved MoS2 precursors for the hydrothermal growth.[27] Flower shape MoS2 nanoflowers were grown directly and uniformly onto the 3DG (Figs. 1c and S2b). The NGQDs was grown on the surface of MoS2/3DG by a one-step electro-deposition process by applying 6 V between MoS2/3DG as the working electrode and Pt foil as the counter electrode [28]. As shown in Fig. 1d, NGQD were electrodeposited directly onto the surfaces of MoS2 nanosheets. In addition, the successful growth of MoS2 on 3DG can be seen from the nucleation sites in Fig. S2a on 3DG surface after 3 h in the hydrothermal reaction.

The detailed morphology of NGQDs decoration on MoS2 and pristine MoS2 was shown in the TEM images as shown in Fig. 2 a–d. From Fig. 2a, fine sheets of MoS2 can be clearly observed on the 3DG. In the high resolution TEM image in Fig. 2b, the interplanar spacing of 6.2 Å can be assigned to the (0 0 2) crystal planes of MoS2 [29]. Fig. 2c shows a low magnification TEM image of the NGQDs. The high resolution TEM image in Fig. 2d exhibits a uniform NGQDs with diameters of 3–5 nm [30].

The crystalline phase and structure of the as-synthesized 3DG, MoS2/3DG and the NGQD@MoS2/3DG nanoarchitectures were performed via X-ray diffraction (XRD) as shown in Fig. 3 a. The intense peak at 26.5° arises from the (0 0 2) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (JCPDS No. 75-1621) [31]. The new major peaks located at 14.4°, 33.0°, 39.5°and 59.0° can be indexed to the (0 0 2), (1 0 0), (1 0 3) and (1 1 0) crystal faces of MoS2 (JCPDS No. 79-1508).[32] In addition, EDX analysis in Fig. S3 shows homogenously distributed elements, Mo, S, and C, which implies the uniform growth of MoS2 on the surfaces of 3DG, and the successful electrodeposition of NGQD on MoS2 nanoflowers. Raman spectroscopy was carried out to further identify the as-synthesized materials (Fig. 3b). There are two peaks at 380 and 408 cm−1 arises from the in-plane E1 2g and out-of-plane A1g modes of MoS2, respectively [33]. There are two peaks at 1573 cm−1 and 2718 cm−1 in 3DG arises from the disorder and defects in the carbon layer, and the in-plane vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in the hexagonal carbon lattice, corresponding to the characteristic G and 2D bands of 3DG respectively [34,35]. The absence of the D band in 3DG is indicative of the high quality and low defect of 3DG produced via CVD method [36].

The electrochemical properties of NGQD@MoS2/3DG are evaluated via electrochemical characterization through cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge cycling. These electrochemical tests were conducted using coin cells with sodium metal as a counter electrode. The control sample of MoS2/3DG and 3DG was tested in the exact same conditions. Fig. 4 a and b shows the charge and discharge curves during cycling in the CV curves of the NGQD@MoS2/3DG and MoS2/3DG electrodes, respectively. The cells were scanned at voltages in the range 0.001–3.0 V, with a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1.

The CV curves of NGQDs@MoS2/3DG for the first cathodic sweep show three cathodic peaks at 1.4 V, 0.7 V and 0.02 V. The cathodic peak at 1.4 V is ascribed to the intercalation of Na+ ions into MoS2, forming NaxMoS2 [37].
(2)
Mo
S 2
+ x N a = N
a x
M o
S 2

The cathodic peak at 0.7 V reveals the following conversion reaction in Eq. (1) above. The cathodic peak at 0.2 V arises from the formation of gel-like solid electrolyte-interface (SEI) film at the interface of the active materials and electrolyte as it is disappeared in the following cycles [38]. In the first anodic sweep, two broad oxidation peaks was observed at 0.5 V and 1.7 V in the first charging process, which corresponded to the oxidation of the Mo nanograins to MoS2 [39]. The effect of NGQD on MoS2 nanosheets are verified in the peak at 1.7 V. After the first cycle, the reduction peak at 0.2 V disappeared and the second and third discharging/charging curves are overlapping. This suggests high reversibility and cycling stability of Na+ storage in the NGQD@MoS2/3DG and MoS2/3DG composite layer. The CV curve of MoS2/3DG is almost same as that of NGQD@MoS2/3DG electrode but with peak slightly shift (Fig. 4b).

Fig. 4c and d shows the 1st, 2nd and 5th charge – discharge curves of the NGQD@MoS2/3DG and MoS2/3DG samples, respectively. The initial discharge curves of the NGQD@MoS2/3DG and MoS2/3DG electrodes consisted of slight plateaus near 1.1 and 0.8 V, respectively. This is due to the distortion of the MoS2 structure during the first discharge and charge cycle. Subsequently, the two potential plateaus seen in the first discharge curves of the two samples were replaced by long slopes between 2.5 and 0.001 V from the second cycle onwards. Failing to fully recover to the pristine MoS2 structure gives rise to the long slopes. The initial discharge and charge capacities of the MoS2/3DG were 385 and 304 mA h g−1, respectively, and the corresponding initial CE was 79.0% calculated by the following formula [40].
(3)
CE =
desodiation
c a p a c i t y
sodiation
c a p a c i t y
× 100 %

The capacity loss of approximately 21.0% during the initial cycle can be attributed to the formation of an SEI layer and the irreversible decomposition of the electrolyte. This phenomenon has also been observed in other nano-sized anode materials as reported in other literature [41–43]. Compared with the MoS2/3DG cell, the initial discharge and charge capacities of the NGQD@MoS2/3DG cell were 638 and 545 mA h g−1, respectively, leading to an initial CE of 85.4%. The improvement of the initial CE can be attributed to the NGQD onto MoS2 nanosheets, lowering the energy barrier of Na+ intercalation into the MoS2 layer [44]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the highest initial CE value reported of MoS2 based anode of SIBs. This high first cycle CE seen in NGQD@MoS2/3DG electrode can effectively reduce the consumption of irreversible sodium ions during the first cycle [45]. In other words, high first cycle CE of anode material can save the usage of cathode material, leading to improved energy density and lower the final cost of the whole cell [14].

The rate capabilities of NGQD@MoS2/3DG MoS2/3DG and 3DG were measured at various current density as shown in Fig. 4e. The MoS2/3DG electrode was able to deliver reversible discharge capacities of 270.2, 221.5, 201.9, 171.5 and 138.7 mA h g−1 at current densities of 50, 100, 200, 500 and 1000 mA g−1 respectively. Owing to the electrodeposition of NGQD on the MoS2/3DG electrode, NGQD@MoS2/3DG was able to deliver significantly higher discharge capacities. The NGQD@MoS2/3DG electrode delivered reversible discharge capacities of 562.7, 511.3, 479.1, 400.5 and 289.4 mA h g−1 at current densities of 50, 100, 200, 500 and 1000 mA g−1 respectively. Furthermore, the capacity was restored back to 562.5 mA h g−1 when the electrode was cycled at current density of 50 mA g−1. This further proves the stability and cyclability of NGQD@MoS2/3DG. The high rate performance in NGQD@MoS2/3DG could be attributed to NGQD lowering the energy barrier for Na+ ion insertion, coupled with the morphology of the anchored MoS2 on 3DG layers, which creates a short diffusion pathway for the exchange of sodium ions and electrons from the MoS2 nanoparticles through the well-connected 3DG network [46]. 3DG serves as a good conductive network and a substrate, having negligible capacity as seen in Fig. S4a and b. Thus, the overall capacity can be attributed completely to MoS2 and NGQD@MoS2.

The cycling stability of NGQD@MoS2/3DG MoS2/3DG and 3DG were measured at a current density of 50 mA g−1 for the first 5 cycles for the activation process, followed by measuring at 500 mA g−1 until 100 cycles (Fig. 4f). Highly reversible and stable cycling was also observed for the NGQD@MoS2/3DG with a capacity retention is 98% after 100 cycles (compared from the 6th cycle onwards), shown in Fig. 4f. The NGQD@MoS2/3DG electrode maintained a capacity of about 400 mAh g−1 throughout the 100 cycles, corresponding to the rate capability curve (Fig. 4e). Furthermore, the initial CE is also consistent with that at 50 mA g−1 of 85.4%, which increased and maintain near 100% in subsequent cycles. This high capacity, highly reversible and stable cycling implies the improved electrochemical performance in sodium storage of MoS2 is attributed to the electrodeposition of NGQD [21].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted for both NGQD@MoS2/3DG and MoS2/3DG to investigate the intrinsic electrochemical kinetics of the two electrodes. The measurements were performed on the coin cells at a semi-charged state (∼2.5 V) across a frequency range of 10 mHz–1 MHz as shown in Fig. 5 . The Nyquist diagram were fitted using the circuitry model as shown in the inset of Fig. 5(b), where Rs is the current collector and electrolyte resistance; Rf and Q1 represent the SEI layer resistance and constant phase element (CPE) respectively; Rct and Q2 represent the charge transfer resistance and double layer capacitor respectively. Zw represents the Warburg impedance related to sodium diffusion [47]. The curves from both electrodes display a similar profile, a small semi-circle in the high frequency region followed by an acute straight line in the low frequency region. The semi-circle region corresponds to a combination of surface film resistance and charge transfer resistance while the acute straight line arises from the Warburg region as well as Na accumulation element [48,49]. The fitted values using the equivalent circuit are presented in Table 1 . The results show that with the electrodeposition of the NGDQ to the MoS2/3DG, the charge transfer resistance of the NGQD@MoS2/3DG electrodes (107.4 Ω) is significantly less than that of the MoS2/3DG electrodes (305.2 Ω).

In summary, a binder free NGQD@MoS2/3DG anode material was synthesized via two steps. First, flower-shaped MoS2 was grown on 3DG via a simple hydrothermal synthesis. Subsequently, NGQDs were electrodeposited on the surface of MoS2/3DG, giving rise NGQD@MoS2/3DG hybrid nanoarchitecture. The NGQD@MoS2/3DG nanoarchitecture exhibits high capacity, long cycle life and exceptional rate capabilities as a binder free anode of SIBs. In specific, it delivers a capacity of 638 mAh g−1 at current density of 50 mA g−1 and an extraordinary high initial CE of 85.4%. Such excellent electrochemical performance is attributed to the synergistic effect between NGQDs, MoS2 and 3DG. The superior battery performance further proves that MoS2 has potential and is a promising anode material for SIBs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Singapore Ministry of Education Academic Research Fund Tier 2 (MOE2015-T2-1-150). Y. Wang acknowledges the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21603192).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2018.04.001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Issues and challenges facing rechargeable lithium batteries
